narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 36 (slightly under the 36.7 average), full-time enrollment plans, and lower-middle family income bracket—and predicted a 67% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 school grade decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school grade decile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. Most critically, your low Year 3 decile ranking—placing you in the bottom tenth of your school—strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, signaling to it that your later academic performance raised serious concerns about exam readiness. In contrast, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, nudging the prediction toward success and showing that this was one of your relative strengths compared to peers. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom tenth (far below the average around the middle of the pack), further reinforced the failure risk by highlighting early struggles that the model sees as persistent red flags. Overall, your academic deciles stood out as weaker than most applicants', outweighing your solid LSAT and typical full-time status in the model's eyes. While your background as an Asian male doesn't directly factor in, the model weighs these performance metrics heavily against historical patterns where similar profiles have higher bar failure rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses or a master's program to improve your decile rankings. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or relevant legal experience could also help reframe your story in a future application. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these gaps—persistence and targeted improvements often turn predictions around.
